quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Mean (x̄)"?

options.text: Standard computer output provides coefficients (b₀, b₁), standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, and R² for interpretation
The arithmetic average of all data values, calculated by summing all values and dividing by n; sensitive to outliers and skewness
If we repeated the sampling process many times, C% of the resulting intervals would capture the true population mean
P(not A) = 1 - P(A); the probability that an event does NOT occur equals one minus the probability it does occur

quiz_questions.explanation: "Mean (x̄)" is defined as: The arithmetic average of all data values, calculated by summing all values and dividing by n; sensitive to outliers and skewness. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Median"?

options.text: P(not A) = 1 - P(A); the probability that an event does NOT occur equals one minus the probability it does occur
The middle value when data are ordered from least to greatest; resistant to outliers and preferred for skewed distributions
The claim we suspect is true and are trying to find evidence for; can be one-sided () or two-sided (≠)
Tests H₀: μ = μ₀ using t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n) with df = n - 1; used when σ is unknown

quiz_questions.explanation: "Median" is defined as: The middle value when data are ordered from least to greatest; resistant to outliers and preferred for skewed distributions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Interquartile range (IQR)"?

options.text: The difference between Q3 (75th percentile) and Q1 (25th percentile), measuring the spread of the middle 50% of data
Retrospective studies look backward at existing data; prospective studies follow subjects forward in time from the start
Random sample or random assignment, n < 10% of population, np₀ ≥ 10 and n(1-p₀) ≥ 10
The magnitude of the difference between the true parameter and the null value; determines the practical importance of a statistically significant result

quiz_questions.explanation: "Interquartile range (IQR)" is defined as: The difference between Q3 (75th percentile) and Q1 (25th percentile), measuring the spread of the middle 50% of data. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Five-number summary"?

options.text: Standard computer output provides coefficients (b₀, b₁), standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, and R² for interpretation
s = √(Σresidual²/(n-2)); estimates the standard deviation of the residuals (σ) with n-2 degrees of freedom
t-procedures are robust to moderate non-normality for large samples but can be misleading for small samples from highly skewed distributions
Minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum—the basis for constructing boxplots and identifying outliers

quiz_questions.explanation: "Five-number summary" is defined as: Minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum—the basis for constructing boxplots and identifying outliers. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Outlier (1.5×IQR rule)"?

options.text: df = k - 1, where k is the number of categories; determines the shape of the chi-square distribution
A data point falling more than 1.5 × IQR below Q1 or above Q3, potentially indicating unusual observations or data errors
Using df = min(n₁-1, n₂-1) when technology is unavailable; produces wider intervals and larger p-values
Increasing n decreases the margin of error proportionally to 1/√n, producing a narrower (more precise) interval

quiz_questions.explanation: "Outlier (1.5×IQR rule)" is defined as: A data point falling more than 1.5 × IQR below Q1 or above Q3, potentially indicating unusual observations or data errors. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The arithmetic average of all data values, calculated by summing all values and dividing by n; sensitive to outliers and skewness"?

options.text: Interpretation of slope CI
Mean (x̄)
Range
Standard normal distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Mean (x̄)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The middle value when data are ordered from least to greatest; resistant to outliers and preferred for skewed distributions"?

options.text: False positive/negative
Homogeneity vs. independence
Response bias
Median

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Median".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The difference between Q3 (75th percentile) and Q1 (25th percentile), measuring the spread of the middle 50% of data"?

options.text: Expected count formula
Z-score
Interquartile range (IQR)
Cumulative distribution function (CDF)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Interquartile range (IQR)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum—the basis for constructing boxplots and identifying outliers"?

options.text: Five-number summary
LINE conditions
Coefficient of variation
Large Counts condition (two samples)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Five-number summary".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A data point falling more than 1.5 × IQR below Q1 or above Q3, potentially indicating unusual observations or data errors"?

options.text: Cramér's V
Outlier (1.5×IQR rule)
Large Counts condition (two samples)
Degrees of freedom (df)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Outlier (1.5×IQR rule)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Descriptive Statistics is accurate?

options.text: A randomized block design combines the benefits of both techniques: blocking controls for known confounders while randomization balances unknown confounders across treatment groups.
Simple random sampling (SRS) gives every possible sample of size n an equal chance of selection, while stratified, cluster, and systematic sampling offer advantages in specific contexts.

The addition rule for mutually exclusive events (P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)) and the general addition rule (P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)) handle unions, while the multiplication rule handles intersections.
Descriptive Statistics provide numerical and graphical summaries that reveal the center, spread, and shape of data distributions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Descriptive Statistics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Descriptive Statistics?

options.text: Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions organize data for two categorical variables and provide the foundation for chi-square analysis.
Data collection methods—including census, survey, and experiment—each have distinct strengths and limitations that affect the validity and generalizability of conclusions.

Data Collection and Types of Variables forms the foundation of statistical analysis by distinguishing between categorical variables (which assign labels or categories) and quantitative variables (which take numerical measurements).
Measures of center include the mean (arithmetic average sensitive to outliers), the median (resistant middle value), and the mode.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Descriptive Statistics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Descriptive Statistics?

options.text: Confidence Intervals for Proportions estimate an unknown population proportion p using the sample proportion p̂ ± z*√(p̂(1-p̂)/n), where z* is the critical value for the desired confidence level.
Discrete Random Variables take countable values (often integers), with each value having an associated probability.
Choosing between mean and median depends on the distribution's shape and presence of outliers, making it critical to examine data graphically before computing summaries.
Range, IQR, and standard deviation each capture different aspects of spread, and choosing the right measure depends on the data's shape and the presence of outliers.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Descriptive Statistics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Descriptive Statistics?

options.text: Descriptive Statistics provide numerical and graphical summaries that reveal the center, spread, and shape of data distributions.
Measures of Variability quantify the spread or dispersion of data, describing how much individual observations deviate from the center.
The p-value measures the probability of observing data as extreme as or more extreme than the sample result, assuming H₀ is true.
Common transformations include log(y) vs. x for exponential relationships and log(y) vs. log(x) for power relationships, with back-transformation needed to interpret predictions in original units.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Descriptive Statistics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Descriptive Statistics?

options.text: Measures of center include the mean (arithmetic average sensitive to outliers), the median (resistant middle value), and the mode.
For independent random variables: Var(X ± Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y)—variances always add, even for differences.
Categorical Data Analysis examines the distribution and relationships of categorical variables using frequency tables, relative frequency tables, and bar charts.
Discrete Random Variables take countable values (often integers), with each value having an associated probability.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Descriptive Statistics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Descriptive Statistics?

options.text: Comparing median household income (resistant to billionaire outliers) versus mean income when evaluating economic policy and wealth distribution
Demonstrating the CLT with simulations: rolling dice (uniform distribution) and plotting the mean of 30 rolls—the distribution of means is approximately normal despite the uniform population
Linearizing the relationship between time and radioactive decay to estimate half-lives using transformed regression
Comparing mean test scores between students taught with traditional lectures vs. active learning methods to evaluate pedagogical effectiveness

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Descriptive Statistics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Descriptive Statistics?

options.text: Portfolio diversification: the variance of a portfolio return equals the weighted sum of individual stock variances (plus covariance terms for non-independent stocks)
Pharmaceutical clinical trials using double-blind, placebo-controlled, randomized designs to test new drug efficacy while meeting FDA requirements
Using boxplots and five-number summaries to compare test score distributions across multiple schools or demographic groups
Identifying confounding variables in public health studies, such as separating the effect of diet from exercise on heart disease rates

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Descriptive Statistics.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Descriptive Statistics applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Pharmaceutical clinical trials using double-blind, placebo-controlled, randomized designs to test new drug efficacy while meeting FDA requirements
Analyzing customer wait times at a restaurant by examining shape, center, and spread to identify service improvement opportunities
Quality control in manufacturing using μ ± 3σ control limits to detect when a production process drifts out of specification
Spam email filtering using conditional probability to classify messages based on the frequency of certain words in spam vs. legitimate emails (Bayesian classification)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Descriptive Statistics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Descriptive Statistics?

options.text: A/B testing in technology companies randomly assigning users to different website designs to determine which produces higher engagement
Medical screening: computing the probability of getting at least one false positive in multiple independent tests using the complement rule: P(at least one) = 1 - P(none)
Astronomy: the power relationship between a planet's orbital period and distance from the sun (Kepler's Third Law: T² ∝ r³) linearized using log-log transformation
Comparing median household income (resistant to billionaire outliers) versus mean income when evaluating economic policy and wealth distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Descriptive Statistics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Descriptive Statistics in practice?

options.text: Tobacco litigation using decades of observational evidence (cohort studies, dose-response relationships) to establish smoking as a cause of lung cancer when experiments were unethical
Using boxplots and five-number summaries to compare test score distributions across multiple schools or demographic groups
Quality control using CLT to construct control charts for process means even when individual measurements follow a skewed distribution
Airport security using sampling distributions to determine how many bags to inspect (sample size) to achieve a desired precision in estimating the rate of prohibited items

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Descriptive Statistics.

Comparing median household income (resistant to billionaire outliers) versus mean income when evaluating economic policy and wealth distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Descriptive Statistics.
